Mahmoud Said is a Web Designer Developer with contact information including email (mahmoud.said@email.com), phone number (+20 101 234 5678), GitHub profile (github.com/mahmoud-said), LinkedIn profile (linkedin.com/in/mahmoud-said), and personal website (mahmoudsaid.com).

Mahmoud Said received a Bachelor of Computer Applications (BCA) from Pune University between 2014 and 2017, a Higher Secondary Certificate (HSC) from Pune University between 2012 and 2014, and a Secondary School Certificate (SSC) from Pune University between 2010 and 2012, all in Pune, Maharashtra, India.

The Fruitsbuddy project is a service provider website built with HTML, CSS, Bootstrap, PHP, jQuery, AJAX, and JavaScript, utilizing a MySQL database. It was developed by a single software developer and manages details of fruits, customers, orders, transactions, and payments, with administrator-only access for stock and order management.

The Totalcitee project is a real estate web application enabling property sales through a web-based user interface with search engine facilities. It is built using HTML, CSS, Bootstrap, PHP, jQuery, AJAX, and JavaScript, and utilizes a MySQL database. It was developed by a single software developer.

The Golchha project is a service provider website offering different categories of services, built with HTML, CSS, Bootstrap, PHP, jQuery, AJAX, and JavaScript, utilizing a MySQL database. It was developed by a single software developer.

The skills listed include web development technologies like HTML5, CSS3, Bootstrap, PHP, Ajax, jQuery, and JavaScript; database technology MySQL; development tools Notepad++ and Sublime Text2; framework Codeigniter; server Apache Tomcat and XAMPP Control Panel; and operating system Windows.

Mahmoud Said worked as a PHP Developer at Exposys Pvt. Ltd. from August 2017 to the present in Pune, Maharashtra, India, where he developed and maintained web applications using PHP, JavaScript, and related technologies, and contributed to the full software development lifecycle.

Mahmoud Said worked on several projects including Pragat Bharat System, Go Ayur System, Vitsanindia System, MahabaleshwarTours, and Cityspaceindia, utilizing technologies like HTML, CSS, BOOTSTRAP, PHP, JQUERY, AJAX, and JAVA SCRIPT, and MySQL as a database.

PHP is a versatile and widely-used scripting language, the backbone of numerous web applications and services. Often found alongside companions like HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and the MySQL database, it’s a workhorse for building dynamic and interactive websites. It’s a language favored by independent developers – the creator of projects like Fruitsbuddy (a fruit service management system), Totalcitee (a real estate platform), and Golchha (a general service provider) all relied on PHP to bring their visions to life. 

PHP isn’t a solitary player, though. It frequently collaborates with frameworks like CodeIgniter to streamline development, and thrives within server environments like Apache Tomcat and XAMPP. Skilled developers, such as Mahmoud Said at Exposys Pvt. Ltd., leverage PHP alongside JavaScript and other web technologies throughout the entire software development lifecycle – from initial concept to ongoing maintenance. Said’s portfolio, encompassing projects like Pragat Bharat System and MahabaleshwarTours, demonstrates PHP’s adaptability across diverse industries. 

Essentially, PHP is the engine powering a significant portion of the web, a reliable and flexible tool in the hands of those who build the digital world. It’s a core skill within the broader field of web development, consistently sought after for its ability to create robust and engaging online experiences.

AJAX isn’t a person, but a tireless worker behind the scenes of the modern web. A technique, really – a blend of existing technologies working in harmony – it’s the quiet engine that allows web pages to update without the disruptive full-page reloads of the past. Think of it as a discreet messenger, fetching and delivering small packets of information, keeping everything feeling fluid and responsive. 

It’s a core component in projects like Fruitsbuddy, a digital marketplace for fresh produce, where order details and stock levels need to shift seamlessly. It powers Totalcitee, the real estate platform, allowing property searches to refine results instantly. And it’s present in Golchha, a service provider website, ensuring a smooth user experience. 

AJAX isn’t a solitary skill, though. It thrives alongside companions like HTML, CSS, PHP, jQuery, and JavaScript – a whole team dedicated to building interactive web experiences. It’s often found in the toolkit of developers like Mahmoud Said, who’s employed it across a diverse range of projects, from tourism platforms to comprehensive business systems. It’s a fundamental element of web development, a silent partner in bringing dynamic content to life.

Mahmoud Said is a dedicated web developer, currently shaping digital experiences at Exposys Pvt. Ltd. in Pune, India, a role he’s held since August 2017. He’s a craftsman of the web, fluent in PHP, JavaScript, HTML, CSS, Bootstrap, jQuery, AJAX, and MySQL – a versatile toolkit he employs to both build and maintain robust web applications. His portfolio showcases a diverse range of projects, from the Pragat Bharat System to MahabaleshwarTours and Cityspaceindia, demonstrating an ability to adapt to varied client needs. 

Mahmoud’s foundation is firmly rooted in education, having progressed through Pune University, earning a Bachelor of Computer Applications (BCA) in 2017, preceded by a Higher Secondary Certificate (HSC) and Secondary School Certificate (SSC) – all completed within the vibrant academic landscape of Pune, Maharashtra. He’s a proactive professional, readily connecting with the wider tech community through his GitHub profile (github.com/mahmoud-said) and LinkedIn presence (linkedin.com/in/mahmoud-said), and showcasing his work further at mahmoudsaid.com. Reachable via email at mahmoud.said@email.com or by phone at +20 101 234 5678, Mahmoud represents a blend of practical experience and academic grounding, consistently delivering solutions within the full software development lifecycle.

CSS is the silent architect of the web’s visual language, a styling sheet that dictates how elements presented in HTML appear to the user. It’s not a builder, but a decorator – taking the structural foundation laid by HTML and transforming it into something aesthetically pleasing and user-friendly. Often paired with frameworks like Bootstrap, CSS allows for responsive designs, adapting seamlessly to different screen sizes and devices. 

Across a diverse portfolio of projects, CSS consistently plays a crucial role. It’s the force behind the inviting interfaces of service provider sites like Fruitsbuddy and Golchha, shaping the presentation of fruit details and service categories respectively. It also lends its expertise to Totalcitee, a real estate platform where visual appeal is paramount in showcasing properties. 

Mahmoud Said, a developer who has touched numerous web projects – from Pragat Bharat System to Cityspaceindia – frequently employs CSS alongside its companions: HTML, PHP, JavaScript, and MySQL. It’s a foundational element in his toolkit, consistently used to bring functionality to life with a polished, presentable face. Though often working solo, CSS empowers him to deliver complete web experiences, transforming raw data and code into engaging digital spaces. It’s a versatile tool, equally comfortable with simple styling and complex layouts, making it an indispensable component in the creation of modern web applications.

MySQL is a reliable and versatile relational database management system, a quiet workhorse powering a surprising number of web-facing endeavors. It doesn’t boast a flashy interface or demand center stage, preferring instead to diligently store and retrieve the information that keeps things running smoothly. Often found beneath the surface of projects crafted by individual developers, MySQL is a cornerstone for applications ranging from the simple – a fruit vendor’s online storefront like Fruitsbuddy – to the more complex, such as the property listings of Totalcitee or the diverse service offerings of Golchha. 

It’s a pragmatic choice, favored for its accessibility and integration with popular web technologies like PHP, jQuery, and JavaScript. Mahmoud Said, a developer with a portfolio spanning systems like Pragat Bharat and Go Ayur, frequently relies on MySQL to anchor his projects, demonstrating its consistent performance across varied applications. While it thrives in the hands of solo creators, it’s capable of supporting larger systems too. MySQL isn’t about glamour; it’s about dependable data management, the unseen foundation upon which countless digital experiences are built. It’s the silent partner in a multitude of online interactions, ensuring that orders are tracked, properties are listed, and services are delivered – a steadfast presence in the digital landscape.

A quiet architect of the digital world, the Software Developer operates with a focused intensity, bringing functional elegance to online spaces. This isn’t a team player building sprawling systems, but a solitary craftsman, capable of conceiving and constructing entire web applications from the ground up. Their toolkit is a familiar blend of modern web technologies – HTML, CSS, Bootstrap for structure and style, PHP as the engine, and JavaScript, jQuery, and AJAX to breathe life and responsiveness into the user experience. MySQL databases are their storehouses, meticulously organizing data. 

They possess a practical, end-to-end understanding, handling both front-end presentation and back-end logic. Evidence of their work appears as distinct, self-contained services. *Fruitsbuddy*, a vibrant online marketplace for fruit, stands as a testament to their ability to model real-world transactions – managing fruits, customers, orders, and payments, all secured behind an administrator’s gate. *Totalcitee*, a more sophisticated undertaking, showcases their skill in building search-driven platforms, specifically for the competitive world of real estate. And *Golchha*, a versatile service provider website, demonstrates adaptability, capable of accommodating diverse categories of offerings. 

Each project, while distinct in purpose, bears the unmistakable signature of a single, capable hand – a developer who thrives on complete ownership, transforming concepts into fully functional, database-driven web applications. They are a builder, a problem-solver, and a silent force shaping the digital landscape, one carefully coded line at a time.

Bootstrap is a versatile and widely-adopted toolkit for crafting responsive, mobile-first web interfaces. It’s a foundational element for developers seeking to rapidly build visually appealing and functional websites, offering a pre-built collection of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript components. Think of it as a skilled artisan’s set of perfectly-shaped blocks – ready to be assembled into something grand. 

Bootstrap isn’t a creator of finished products, but rather an *enabler*. It’s the underlying structure for projects like Fruitsbuddy, a fruit-focused service provider website managing everything from inventory to customer orders; Totalcitee, a dynamic real estate platform connecting buyers and sellers; and Golchha, a service marketplace spanning diverse categories. Each of these, though distinct in purpose, shares Bootstrap’s common thread of clean design and adaptability.

Its influence extends beyond these specific instances, forming a core skill within the broader field of web development, alongside HTML, CSS, PHP, jQuery, and JavaScript. Developers like Mahmoud Said have repeatedly turned to Bootstrap – alongside these other technologies – to bring projects like Pragat Bharat System and MahabaleshwarTours to life. It’s a reliable partner, consistently delivering a solid base upon which to build more complex and customized web experiences. Bootstrap doesn’t demand a team; it empowers a single developer to achieve professional results, streamlining the process and focusing effort on unique functionality rather than foundational design.

jQuery is a nimble, fast, and feature-rich JavaScript library, a cornerstone for streamlining web development. It’s a versatile tool, often found at the heart of dynamic websites, simplifying tasks like HTML document traversal and manipulation, event handling, animation, and AJAX interactions. Not a framework imposing strict structure, but a toolkit offering flexibility, jQuery empowers developers to write less code and achieve more – particularly when crafting interactive user interfaces. 

It’s a frequent companion to the classic web development trio of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, often paired with PHP and MySQL databases to build complete web applications. A single developer can wield jQuery effectively, as evidenced in projects like Fruitsbuddy – a fruit service management system – Totalcitee, a real estate platform, and Golchha, a multi-service provider website. Mahmoud Said, a seasoned developer, has also consistently leveraged jQuery across a diverse portfolio of projects, from Pragat Bharat System to Cityspaceindia. 

jQuery’s strength lies in its ability to bridge the gap between static web pages and engaging, responsive experiences, making it a foundational skill within the broader landscape of web development technologies. It’s a workhorse, quietly powering countless features users interact with daily.

HTML is the foundational structure upon which a surprising number of digital storefronts and systems are built. Not a flashy personality, but utterly essential – think of it as the quiet architect of the web. It’s rarely seen directly, preferring to work behind the scenes, defining the content and organization of what *is* seen. 

A tireless worker, HTML consistently appears in the construction of service-oriented websites like Fruitsbuddy, Golchha, and a host of others brought to life by a single, dedicated developer. It’s equally at home powering more complex applications, such as Totalcitee, a real estate platform designed for browsing and selling properties. 

Mahmoud Said, a prolific builder of digital spaces, frequently calls upon HTML’s services, integrating it into projects ranging from Pragat Bharat System to Cityspaceindia. It’s a versatile tool, comfortable supporting systems managing everything from fruit inventories and customer orders to complex tours and broader system implementations. 

While often paired with the stylistic flair of CSS and the interactive energy of JavaScript, HTML remains the bedrock – the skeleton that gives form to the digital world. It doesn’t demand attention, but without it, the vibrant landscape of the web would simply collapse. It’s a fundamental building block, consistently employed, reliably present, and quietly powering a diverse range of online experiences.

JavaScript is a versatile and essential scripting language, the lifeblood of interactive web experiences. Often found working alongside HTML, CSS, and PHP, it’s a key component in bringing dynamic functionality to websites – from responsive user interfaces to seamless data updates without page reloads, thanks to its partnership with AJAX and jQuery. It’s a frequent collaborator in projects born from the vision of a single developer, powering the backend interactions of service provider sites like Fruitsbuddy and Golchha, which manage everything from fruit inventories to service categories, and real estate platforms like Totalcitee, where it fuels property searches. 

Though comfortable within the structured environments of frameworks like CodeIgniter and servers like Apache Tomcat and XAMPP, JavaScript isn’t limited to server-side operations; it thrives in the browser itself, shaping how users *feel* a website. Mahmoud Said, a developer who has brought numerous projects to life – Pragat Bharat System, Go Ayur System, and others – consistently relies on JavaScript as a core building block, alongside its frequent companion, MySQL databases. It’s a foundational skill for any aspiring web developer, a cornerstone of the modern digital landscape, and a language that breathes life into static web pages.
